en.extract:
The golden-eared tanager (Tangara chrysotis) is a species of bird in the tanager family Thraupidae. It is found in the eastern Andes of Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru where its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.


Taxonomy and systematics
The golden-eared tanager was first described as Calliste chrysotis by Bernard du Bus de Gisignies in 1846, on the basis of a specimen collected from Peru. The generic name Tangara comes from the Tupí word tangara, which means dancer. The specific name chrysotis is from the Ancient Greek χρυσος (khrusos), meaning golden, and ωτις (otis), meaning eared. Golden-eared tanager is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC). Other names for the species include golden eared tanager.
The golden-eared tanager is one of 27 species in the genus Tangara. Within the genus, it is put in a species group with the blue-whiskered tanager, green-and-gold tanager, emerald tanager, golden tanager, silver-throated tanager, saffron-crowned tanager, and flame-faced tanager. The golden-eared tanager is sister to a clade formed by the rest of the species in this group. This placement has been supported by DNA evidence. It has no subspecies.

The '''golden-eared tanager''' ('''''Tangara chrysotis''''') is a [[species]] of [[bird]] in the tanager [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Thraupidae]]. It is found in the eastern [[Andes]] of [[Bolivia]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]] and [[Peru]] where its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Ridgely|first1=Robert S.|last2=Tudor|first2=Guy|title=Field Guide to the Songbirds of South America: The Passerines|date=2009|publisher=University of Texas Press|isbn=978-0-292-71748-0|pages=588–589|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gC_A2fpBl1gC&q=Tangara+chrysotis&pg=RA2-PR58|access-date=9 February 2018|language=en}}</ref>

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The golden-eared tanager was first described as ''Calliste chrysotis'' by [[Bernard du Bus de Gisignies]] in 1846, on the basis of a specimen collected from [[Peru]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Du Bus de Gisignies|first=Bernard|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/220129|title=Esquisses ornithologiques: descriptions et figures d'oiseaux nouveaux ou peu connus|last2=Vandale|first2=A.|last3=Severeyns|first3=G.|last4=Keghel|first4=Joseph de|publisher=A. Vandale|year=1845|location=Brussels|language=fr}}</ref> The generic name ''Tangara'' comes from the [[Tupi language|Tupí]] word ''tangara'', which means dancer. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''chrysotis'' is from the [[Ancient Greek]] χρυσος (''khrusos''), meaning golden, and ωτις (''otis''), meaning eared.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Jobling|first=James A.|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names|publisher=[[Christopher Helm]]|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-3326-2|location=London|pages=106, 379}}</ref> Golden-eared tanager is the official [[common name]] designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Tanagers and allies – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/tanagers/|access-date=2021-09-04|language=en-US}}</ref> Other names for the species include golden eared tanager.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Tangara parzudakii (Flame-faced Tanager) – Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=E07FBD8E5E26BA75|access-date=2021-09-04|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref>

The golden-eared tanager is one of 27 species in the genus ''[[Tangara (bird)|Tangara]]''. Within the genus, it is put in a [[species group]] with the [[blue-whiskered tanager]], [[green-and-gold tanager]], [[emerald tanager]], [[golden tanager]], [[silver-throated tanager]], [[saffron-crowned tanager]], and [[flame-faced tanager]]. The golden-eared tanager is [[Sister species|sister]] to a [[clade]] formed by the rest of the species in this group. This placement has been supported by [[DNA]] evidence.<ref name=":02">{{Citation|last=Nogueda|first=Ingrid|title=Golden-eared Tanager (Tangara chrysotis)|date=2020-03-04|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/goetan1/1.0/introduction|work=Birds of the World|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.goetan1.01|access-date=2021-11-01|last2=Burns|first2=Kevin J.|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> It has no subspecies.<ref name=":0" />
